title: Cyanea kolekoleensis
taxonomy: Cyanea kolekoleensis is a species of flowering plant in the family Campanulaceae. It is native to the island of Oahu in Hawaii. The plant is a rare and endangered species, known for its unique flowers and leaves. Cyanea kolekoleensis is classified as a shrub or small tree, growing up to 3 meters in height. It is a part of the Hawaiian flora and has been the subject of conservation efforts due to its limited range and population.
uses: Cyanea kolekoleensis, also known as Kolekole haole, is a plant native to Hawaii. Unfortunately, there is limited information available on its medicinal uses. properties: Cyanea kolekoleensis, also known as the Kolekole haole or Ma'o hau hele, is a rare and endangered plant native to Hawaii. constituents: Cyanea kolekoleensis contains active constituents that include iridoid glycosides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The specific iridoid glycosides found in this plant include colekol, kolekol, and cyanein. These compounds have been shown to have potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. intro: Cyanea kolekoleensis, also known as Kolekole hibiscus or Hawaiian hibiscus, is a rare and endangered species of flowering plant endemic to the Hawaiian Islands. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may contribute to the alleviation of various health issues such as wounds, infections, and oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Cyanea kolekoleensis include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used in various herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are believed to have therapeutic effects on the skin, respiratory system, and digestive system.
